scenario_results.reason: The lane produced a plant analytics brief tied to named production, lot, inventory, supplier, and logistics records without implying release or disclosure signoff.
Metric denominator ambiguity blocked confident publication and triggered a minimum-definition request instead of silent normalization.
Incomplete and stale inventory data was surfaced as low-confidence risk with explicit missing-location impact on the metric package.
Conflicting ERP, MES, and QMS evidence was preserved as an unresolved reconciliation issue rather than collapsed into one authoritative outcome.
The lane refused an unsupported causal claim and limited the output to descriptive trend language plus evidence gaps.
Release and disclosure pressure did not override named quality holds, deviation records, or escalation requirements.
Traceability-aware analysis kept ingredient-lot genealogy, downstream scope, and record hierarchy explicit without unsupported containment claims.
Quarantined inventory was segmented from available metrics and release authority was routed back to quality owners.
Supplier substitution pressure was routed to procurement, supplier quality, and change-control owners without unsupported approval.
Food-and-beverage terminology was normalized to specific plant artifacts and systems instead of generic dashboard language.
Missing tenant facts and source precedence produced a blocked-on-prerequisites result rather than implied comparability or guessed source hierarchy.
Tenant-specific adaptation was recorded explicitly, baseline definitions were preserved, and the report avoided silent replacement of standard logic.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against a standard 0.92 threshold for food-and-beverage metric definition, traceability-aware analysis, supplier and release-boundary handling, and disclosure-safe escalation.
The package remains explicitly validated as a spec pack and does not claim retained-implementation runtime authority.
